system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 10: iii7 vi7 II7 V7 (and I vi7 II7) V7

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 10 — iii7 vi7 II7 V7 (and I vi7 II7) V7

_Pages 74-77._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## iii7 vi7 II7 V7 derivation method

> The quickest way for you to come up with some good cxamples of this progression is to go back to your iii? vi7 ii7 V7
> progressions and raise the 3rd (one fret higher) in the ii? types, thereby making them I]7’s.

**Significance (p. 74).** Core technique for deriving II7 voicings from ii7 shapes.

## II7 with two 3rds — fingering rule

> If there are two 3rds in a
> ii7 type, try raising the 3rd that is the lower in pitch of the two, and just leave the other one alone; this will create a
>
> 117#9 chord in many cases.

**Significance (p. 74).** Specific fingering rule for ambiguous cases; names II7#9 result.

## iim7/11 soprano exception

> If you are dealing with a iim7/11 with its 11th in the soprano (highest pitch) then none
> of these ideas will work very well.

**Significance (p. 74).** Boundary condition where conversion method does not apply.

## VI7 derivation from vi7

> Actually some of them were (that is, the 3rd was raised in the vi7, thereby creating the V17), but most of them
> were derived in other ways, specifically to produce more desirable sounds.

**Significance (p. 74).** Same mechanism applied to vi7 to produce VI7.

## VI7 derivation — optional exercise

> if you are curious, you may wish
> to go back to the iii7 vi7 ii7 V7’s and raise the 3rd on the vi7, as explained above, just to see what you come up with.

**Significance (p. 74).** Self-directed exercise for deriving VI7 voicings.

## V7 resolution practice directive

> Remember to try resolving the V7’s to I, occasionally.

**Significance (p. 74).** Standing rule for practicing V7 resolutions.

## iii7 vi7 II7 V7 chapter scope

> The next harmonic formula to concern us is going to be iii? vi7 17 V7 (sometimes 17 will be used in place of ili7).

**Significance (p. 74).** Defines chapter's governing harmonic formula and substitution equivalence.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
